title: Why being praised feels dangerous after emotional abuse
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The compliment that lands like a threat 

 Someone tells you you did a good job. Maybe it's your boss. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a partner looking at you...
metaDescription:
The compliment that lands like a threat 

 Someone tells you you did a good job. Maybe it's your boss. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a partner looking a...
htmlContent:
The compliment that lands like a threat

Someone tells you you did a good job. Maybe it's your boss. Maybe it's a friend. Maybe it's a partner looking at you with soft eyes. And instead of feeling warm, you feel something tighten in your chest. Your stomach drops. Your shoulders go rigid. A voice in your head starts scanning for the catch.

I've been there. I know that feeling. It's confusing as hell. Here's the thing: your nervous system isn't broken. It's doing exactly what it learned to do.

When you've been through emotional abuse, praise doesn't feel like a reward. It feels like a trap. Because in the environment where you learned to survive, compliments were never free. They came with strings. They were followed by criticism. They were used to build you up so the fall could hurt more. Your brain learned that being seen meant being targeted.

Think about it. Emotional abuse often operates on a cycle. There's the idealization phase where you're told you're wonderful, special, the best thing that ever happened. Then comes the devaluation. The same person who praised you now tells you you're too sensitive, you're selfish, you're the problem. The praise wasn't real. It was a setup.

Your nervous system remembers this. Even if your conscious mind knows this person is safe, your body doesn't. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say your vagus nerve is detecting cues of safety or danger below your awareness. When someone praises you, your system might detect a mismatch. The words say "safe" but your history says "danger." And your body chooses the history every time.

So you deflect. You minimize. You change the subject. You tell yourself you don't deserve it. You wait for the other shoe to drop. Right? I've done all of it.

What your nervous system is actually doing

Let me get specific about what happens in your body when praise triggers you. Because it's not just in your head. It's in your nervous system, your endocrine system, your whole physiology.

When you hear praise, your brain runs a rapid threat assessment. The prefrontal cortex, your rational thinking center, might register the words as positive. But the amygdala, your threat detector, is faster. It doesn't listen to words. It listens to tone, facial expression, body language, and most , pattern recognition. If your history includes people who used praise to manipulate you, your amygdala flags the whole situation as potentially dangerous.

This activates your sympathetic nervous system, your fight or flight response. Or it might activate your dorsal vagal system, the freeze response. You might feel agitated, restless, like you need to escape the room. Or you might feel numb, disconnected, like you're watching yourself from far away. Both are valid trauma responses.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body. It's not a memory you can think your way out of. It's a physiological state that gets triggered by reminders. Praise becomes a reminder. Not because you're weak or broken, but because your system is trying to protect you from what it learned was inevitable.

Here's what that protection looks like in real life. You get praised at work. You immediately start working harder to prove you're not a fraud. You get praised by a partner. You start scanning for signs they're about to withdraw or criticize. You get praised by a friend. You deflect with a joke or self-deprecation. You never actually let the praise land. You never let yourself feel it.

And that's exhausting. I know. I've spent years practicing not receiving good things because my system was so convinced they were dangerous.

The shame layer that makes it worse

Here's where it gets really tricky. Not only does praise trigger your threat response, but the threat response itself triggers shame. You know, logically, that you should be able to accept a compliment. You see other people doing it. They say "thank you" and move on. They don't spiral. They don't dissociate. So when you can't do that, you add a layer of self-criticism.

You tell yourself you're broken. You tell yourself you're too damaged to function normally. You tell yourself there's something wrong with you that makes you unable to receive love or appreciation.

Stay with me here. That shame is not truth. It's a conditioned response. It's the voice of the person who hurt you, internalized so deeply that you can't tell where they end and you begin.

Pete Walker, who wrote about complex PTSD and emotional flashbacks, described this as the inner critic. The critic doesn't just attack you for failing. It attacks you for succeeding, for being seen, for having any visibility at all. Because visibility was dangerous. Being noticed meant being targeted. The critic learned to keep you small and invisible as a survival strategy.

So when someone praises you, the critic panics. It says "Don't let them see you. Don't let them know you're good at anything. They'll use it against you. They'll expect more. They'll watch you fail." The critic is trying to protect you by making you disappear.

But you don't have to keep living that way. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. It takes time. It takes practice. It takes a lot of uncomfortable moments where you let praise sit in your body without pushing it away. But it's possible.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. It explains so much about why your body reacts the way it does. It helped me understand that my responses weren't character flaws. They were survival adaptations.

Learning to let praise land, slowly

So what do you do about this? How do you start receiving praise when your whole system screams that it's dangerous?

First, you stop trying to fix it overnight. This is not a problem you can think your way out of. It's a nervous system pattern that needs to be retrained slowly, gently, with a lot of self-compassion.

Start small. Really small. Someone compliments your shirt. You say "thank you" and notice what happens in your body. You don't have to believe the compliment. You don't have to feel good about it. You just have to notice. Notice the tightness in your chest. Notice the urge to deflect. Notice the voice that says "they're just being nice" or "they want something from you." Just notice. Don't fight it.

Then breathe. Take a slow exhale. This activates your vagus nerve and tells your system it's okay to come out of threat mode. Deb Dana, a clinician who works extensively with Polyvagal Theory, calls this "glimmering" - finding small moments of safety and letting them register.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. It addresses how emotional neglect in childhood creates patterns where we don't know how to receive positive attention. It's been incredibly validating for many people I've worked with.

Next, practice with safe people. If you have one person in your life who feels genuinely safe, ask them to help you practice. Tell them "I'm working on receiving praise. Can you give me a small compliment and let me practice saying thank you without deflecting?" This might feel awkward. It might feel vulnerable. But it's how new neural pathways form.

Wild, right? That you have to practice something that seems so simple. But that's trauma recovery. It's practicing the things that should have been natural if your development hadn't been interrupted by abuse.

Your nervous system might also need some biochemical support. Trauma depletes certain nutrients and neurotransmitters. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the parasympathetic nervous system, which helps you shift out of threat mode. Another option is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link), an amino acid that promotes calm without sedation. These aren't cures, but they can make the process a little gentler.

What it means when you can finally say "thank you"

I want to be honest with you. Learning to receive praise doesn't mean you'll ever love it. It doesn't mean you'll seek it out or become someone who thrives on external validation. For many of us, that's just not our wiring. And that's okay.

What it does mean is that praise stops being a trigger. It stops sending you into a tailspin of anxiety, shame, or dissociation. It becomes neutral. Maybe even slightly pleasant. You can hear it, acknowledge it, and move on with your day without it hijacking your nervous system.

That's the goal. Not to become a praise junkie. Not to need constant validation. Just to be able to receive a good thing without it feeling like a threat.

I'm not fully there yet. I still feel that tightness sometimes. I still have moments where I deflect or minimize. But I can also feel the difference. I can feel the moments when I let it land, when I say "thank you" and actually mean it, when I let myself be seen without preparing for the blow.

Your nervous system learned to protect you in a world where praise was dangerous. That was smart. That was adaptive. That kept you alive. But you're not in that world anymore. And your nervous system can learn that too. It just needs time, patience, and a lot of gentle practice.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. That's your system starting to trust that maybe, just maybe, it's safe to be seen.

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk
